intro:
Buying too little paint means a second trip to the store and the risk of a color mismatch from a different batch. Buying too much wastes money and creates disposal problems. Our paint calculator precisely estimates the gallons you need based on your room's dimensions, ceiling height, number of coats, and the paint's coverage rate.

The calculator covers all four walls by computing the perimeter times ceiling height for total wall area, then divides by your paint's coverage rate (typically 350–400 sq ft per gallon for most interior paints). Most rooms require 2 coats for full coverage and vibrant color, especially when changing from a dark to light color or using a bright accent color.

For most standard 12×14 rooms with 8-foot ceilings, you will need about 1.5–2 gallons for walls with 2 coats. Always verify coverage on the paint can label, as it varies by brand and sheen level.
howItWorks: Enter the room length, width, ceiling height (default 8 ft), number of coats (default 2), and paint coverage per gallon (default 400 sq ft — adjust based on your can label). The calculator computes total wall area, applies the waste factor, and shows gallons needed rounded up to the nearest whole number.
interpretationGuide: Gallons needed accounts for your specified number of coats. Always round up to the nearest quart or gallon when purchasing. Buy from the same batch (same lot numbers) to ensure color consistency. Store leftover paint in sealed containers for touch-ups.

faqs.question: How much paint do I need for a 12×14 room?
faqs.answer: For a 12×14 room with 8-ft ceilings: Wall area = 2×(12+14)×8 = 416 sq ft. At 400 sq ft/gallon with 2 coats, you need ~2 gallons plus trim paint. Subtract area for doors (about 20 sq ft each) and large windows if desired.

faqs.question: Should I include the ceiling in my paint calculation?
faqs.answer: This calculator computes wall paint only. For the ceiling, add room length × width to your calculation. At 400 sq ft/gallon, a 12×14 ceiling (168 sq ft) requires less than half a gallon per coat. Ceiling paint is typically flat white and sold separately.

faqs.question: How much paint does a gallon typically cover?
faqs.answer: Most interior latex paints cover 350–400 sq ft per coat on smooth surfaces. Highly porous surfaces (drywall texture, unprimed wood) absorb more paint and may reduce coverage to 250–300 sq ft. Higher-sheen paints (semi-gloss, gloss) tend to cover slightly less than flat or eggshell.

faqs.question: Do I need primer?
faqs.answer: Primer is needed when painting over bare drywall, bare wood, very dark colors, stains, or water damage. Priming increases coverage and improves adhesion. Many modern paints are 'paint and primer in one,' which can eliminate a separate primer coat for straightforward repaints over similar colors.

faqs.question: What paint sheen should I choose for different rooms?
faqs.answer: Flat/matte: ceilings, low-traffic adult bedrooms (hides imperfections). Eggshell: living rooms, dining rooms. Satin: hallways, children's rooms (cleanable). Semi-gloss: kitchens, bathrooms, trim (most moisture-resistant). High-gloss: doors, cabinets, furniture (very durable, shows imperfections).

faqs.question: How many gallons for an entire house?
faqs.answer: Average 1,500 sq ft house: roughly 8–10 gallons for interior walls (2 coats), plus 2 gallons for ceilings, plus 1 gallon for trim. Larger open-concept homes need more; highly divided floor plans need similar amounts. Get multiple bids and calculate room by room for accuracy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between latex and oil-based paint?
faqs.answer: Latex (water-based) paint: faster drying (1–2 hours), easy cleanup with water, flexible, lower VOCs, better for most interior applications. Oil-based paint: harder and more durable finish, better for trim and cabinets, but requires solvents for cleanup, takes 8–24 hours to dry, and has higher VOCs.

faqs.question: How do I calculate paint for trim and doors?
faqs.answer: Door: approximately 20 sq ft per side. Window: approximately 6–10 sq ft. Baseboards: 1 sq ft per linear foot. For an average room, trim typically needs 1 quart per room with 2 coats. Use semi-gloss or gloss for trim for durability.

faqs.question: Should I subtract doors and windows from wall area?
faqs.answer: For a conservative estimate, subtract approximately 20 sq ft for each standard door and 15 sq ft for each average window from your total wall area. For rough estimates, many painters skip this subtraction and simply buy one extra quart as buffer.

faqs.question: How do I store leftover paint?
faqs.answer: Seal the can tightly, bang the lid with a rubber mallet, and store upside down (creates a paint seal). Keep in a climate-controlled area (not a freezing garage). Label with room name, color code, and purchase date. Properly stored latex paint lasts 2–5 years. Check for lumps or foul smell before use.
